title: Durable Execution: Workflows That Survive Anything
topic: durable execution and workflow engines: checkpointing, event sourcing and replay, idempotency, sagas and compensation, exactly-once illusions over at-least-once delivery, and how engines like Temporal, Oban, and Ash Reactor implement them
summary: A first-principles tour of durable execution for backend engineers, told in the calm, failure-first, back-of-the-envelope voice of Jim Gray. It starts where all reliability starts — machines crash, networks partition, disks lie — and builds up from the append-only log (WAL and ARIES) to the mechanisms that let a program survive its own death: checkpointing, event sourcing and deterministic replay; idempotency and the exactly-once illusion over at-least-once delivery; durable timers; and sagas with compensation for the failures replay can't hide. Every mechanism is introduced by the specific failure it exists to survive and the guarantee it does — and does not — make, then grounded in how Temporal, Oban, and Ash Reactor actually implement it.

concepts.title: Durable Timers & the Passage of Time
The Engines: Temporal, Oban & Ash Reactor

images.alt: A frontispiece engraving: a ledger book whose written lines rise up and rebuild a machine of gears and workflows
images.caption: The log is the ground truth; the running machine is only its latest reading.
images.prompt: A refined frontispiece illustration in the style of a classic printed book's engraved plate, warm aged-paper palette (cream, sepia, a single restrained oxblood-red accent). Centre: a large open ledger book with ruled lines and neat hand-entered rows; from its pages, fine engraved lines rise and reassemble into a delicate machine of interlocking gears, arrows and small workflow boxes hovering above the book, as if the machine is being rebuilt from the written record. A faint broken/cracked gear at the edge suggests a crash, with a thin thread of lines already re-forming it. Cross-hatch engraving texture, elegant and restrained, generous margins, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1350x900px, 3:2 landscape; compose with the ledger-and-machine centred and safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 3:2 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: Three ways a system fails, drawn as three small engraved vignettes: a stopped machine, a cut wire, and a warped disk
images.caption: The three enemies: the machine stops, the wire is cut, the disk lies.
images.prompt: An elegant engraved triptych in classic printed-book style on warm aged paper (cream and sepia with a single restrained oxblood-red accent). Three small vignettes side by side, each framed by a thin ruled border: (1) a mechanical clockwork machine frozen mid-motion with a snapped mainspring — 'the machine stops'; (2) a telegraph/telephone wire cut clean through with the two ends drifting apart and a message envelope falling — 'the wire is cut'; (3) a warped, buckled phonograph-style disk returning a smudged, wrong reading — 'the disk lies'. Fine cross-hatch engraving texture, calm and scholarly, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose the three vignettes centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: A hand re-reading a ledger to rebuild a scene of stacked blocks exactly as it stood before
images.caption: Replay: re-read the log from the top and the state rebuilds itself, step for step.
images.prompt: A warm, scholarly engraved illustration in classic printed-book style on aged paper (cream, sepia, single oxblood-red accent). A hand runs a finger down the ruled lines of an open ledger; from each line, a translated action rises to rebuild a small tower of labelled blocks on a table to the right — the tower reconstructed exactly as an earlier faded 'ghost' outline shows it once stood. A few lines near the bottom are not yet read, and the corresponding top blocks are still faint/half-formed, showing the rebuild in progress. One block is marked with a small tick to show a completed step whose result is simply remembered, not redone. Fine cross-hatch engraving, elegant and restrained, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: A chain of booking stamps being undone in reverse by matching cancellation stamps
images.caption: A saga unwinds in reverse: each committed step met by its compensation.
images.prompt: An elegant engraved illustration in classic printed-book style on warm aged paper (cream, sepia, single restrained oxblood-red accent). A left-to-right chain of three rubber-stamp impressions on a ledger page — a plane (flight booked), a building (hotel booked), and a car — where the car stamp has failed and smudged. Above, a reverse arrow sweeps right-to-left, and over the hotel and plane stamps sit matching 'cancelled' counter-stamps in oxblood red, undoing them in reverse order. A small note beside the plane shows a faint residue mark, hinting that compensation offsets rather than perfectly erases. Fine cross-hatch engraving texture, scholarly and calm, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.
